Group A — Explore & Plot: Deep Dive into the Numbers

Author

Workshop Participant

In this session we go deep on the raw numbers — distributions, skew, suspicious values — then build plots and re-make one plot three times to learn what each tweak reveals.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(moments)

sample_ids   <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/00_sample_ids_yogurt.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
participants <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/01_participant_metadata_yogurt.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
qpcr         <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/02_qpcr_results_yogurt.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
luminex      <- read_csv("../../data/group_a_yogurt/03_luminex_results_yogurt.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

qjoined <- sample_ids |> left_join(qpcr, by = "sample_id")
ljoined <- sample_ids |> left_join(luminex, by = "sample_id")

1. Distributions — participant metadata

Age

Age is roughly symmetric, centred around 31, range 24–37.

Code 
summary(participants$age)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  24.00   28.00   31.00   30.73   33.00   37.00
Code 
cat("SD:", sd(participants$age), "| Skewness:", skewness(participants$age))
SD: 3.286813 | Skewness: -0.04784677
Code 
hist(participants$age, breaks = 10, col = "steelblue",
     main = "Age distribution", xlab = "Age (years)", ylab = "Count")

Days since last sex

This one is right-skewed (skewness = 2.46). Most values are 1–10 days, but a few go up to 48. Worth asking: are these extreme values real, or data entry errors?

Code 
summary(participants$days_since_last_sex)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   1.00    3.00    5.00    8.49   10.50   48.00
Code 
cat("SD:", sd(participants$days_since_last_sex), "| IQR:", IQR(participants$days_since_last_sex))
SD: 9.390149 | IQR: 7.5
Code 
cat("Values > 14 days:", sum(participants$days_since_last_sex > 14))
Values > 14 days: 6
Code 
cat(" | > 30 days:", sum(participants$days_since_last_sex > 30))
 | > 30 days: 2
Code 
hist(participants$days_since_last_sex, breaks = 15, col = "coral",
     main = "Days since last sex", xlab = "Days", ylab = "Count")

2. Distributions — qPCR (bacterial DNA)

All three qPCR variables are heavily right-skewed — a few samples have very high values while most are low.

Total bacterial load

Code 
summary(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria)
     Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
     4252    264994   2236996   9351185   7532202 126400280
Code 
cat("SD:", sd(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria), "| Skewness:", round(skewness(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria), 2))
SD: 19359669 | Skewness: 3.55
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria, breaks = 30, col = "darkgreen",
     main = "Total bacteria", xlab = "DNA copies")
hist(log10(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria + 1), breaks = 30, col = "darkgreen",
     main = "Total bacteria (log10)", xlab = "log10(DNA copies + 1)")

The log10 transform reveals a bimodal distribution — two peaks. That’s interesting and worth looking into by group.

L. crispatus

Code 
summary(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus)
     Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
      0.0       0.0       0.0   95443.0      48.2 6572278.4
Code 
cat("Skewness:", round(skewness(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus), 2))
Skewness: 9.41
Code 
cat("Zeros:", sum(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus == 0), "/", nrow(qpcr),
    "=", round(mean(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus == 0) * 100, 1), "%")
Zeros: 66 / 102 = 64.7 %
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus, breaks = 30, col = "purple",
     main = "L. crispatus", xlab = "DNA copies")
hist(log10(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus + 1), breaks = 30, col = "purple",
     main = "L. crispatus (log10)", xlab = "log10(DNA copies + 1)")

64.7% of samples have zero L. crispatus. The log-scale plot shows data clusters at zero (left bar) and then a long tail of non-zero values.

L. iners

Code 
summary(qpcr$qpcr_iners)
     Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
      0.0      22.3   32352.7  291916.1  302450.4 3854096.8
Code 
cat("SD:", sd(qpcr$qpcr_iners), "| Skewness:", round(skewness(qpcr$qpcr_iners), 2))
SD: 653410.4 | Skewness: 3.8
Code 
cat("Zeros:", sum(qpcr$qpcr_iners == 0), "/", nrow(qpcr))
Zeros: 8 / 102
Code 
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(qpcr$qpcr_iners, breaks = 30, col = "orange",
     main = "L. iners", xlab = "DNA copies")
hist(log10(qpcr$qpcr_iners + 1), breaks = 30, col = "orange",
     main = "L. iners (log10)", xlab = "log10(DNA copies + 1)")

Only 8 out of 102 samples have zero L. iners — almost everyone has some. The distribution is still heavily skewed.

3. Suspicious values

Extreme outliers in qPCR

Using the “3 × IQR beyond Q3” rule:

Code 
check <- function(x, nm) {
  q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75); q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25)
  thr <- q3 + 3 * (q3 - q1)
  extreme <- x[x > thr]
  cat(nm, "- threshold:", round(thr, 1), "| count:", length(extreme), "\n")
  if (length(extreme) > 0) {
    print(sort(extreme, decreasing = TRUE))
  }
}
check(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria, "Total bacteria")
Total bacteria - threshold: 29333827 | count: 9 
[1] 126400280  78581750  65059240  65059240  65059240  54468164  39892616
[8]  39833824  35260360
Code 
check(qpcr$qpcr_crispatus, "L. crispatus")
L. crispatus - threshold: 192.6 | count: 22 
 [1] 6572278.400 1040093.880  539327.800  518340.340  308138.540  187931.720
 [7]  147887.680  116559.940   73925.132   65541.492   57770.704   30850.400
[13]   30850.400   21060.826   11636.088    4586.006    2011.896    2011.896
[19]    1119.092    1119.092     812.958     812.958
Code 
check(qpcr$qpcr_iners, "L. iners")
L. iners - threshold: 1209735 | count: 6 
[1] 3854097 3670552 2479934 2350547 1555847 1555847

Total bacteria has 9 extreme values — are these from one group or time point? Let’s check:

Code 
qjoined |>
  filter(qpcr_bacteria > quantile(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria, 0.75) +
         3 * IQR(qpcr$qpcr_bacteria)) |>
  select(sample_id, arm, time_point, qpcr_bacteria)
# A tibble: 9 × 4
  sample_id arm            time_point qpcr_bacteria
  <chr>     <chr>          <chr>              <dbl>
1 UNCH048   unchanged_diet baseline        39892616
2 YOG069    yogurt         baseline        78581750
3 UNCH072   unchanged_diet baseline        54468164
4 UNCH077   unchanged_diet baseline        39833824
5 YOG078    yogurt         baseline       126400280
6 YOG080    yogurt         baseline        65059240
7 YOG086    yogurt         baseline        35260360
8 UNCH100   unchanged_diet baseline        65059240
9 UNCH101   unchanged_diet baseline        65059240

Luminex: out-of-range measurements

Code 
# Which cytokines are most affected?
luminex |>
  count(cytokine, limits) |>
  group_by(cytokine) |>
  mutate(pct = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1)) |>
  filter(limits == "out of range") |>
  arrange(desc(pct))
# A tibble: 9 × 4
# Groups:   cytokine [9]
  cytokine limits           n   pct
  <chr>    <chr>        <int> <dbl>
1 IL-10    out of range   102 100  
2 IFN-Y    out of range   101  99  
3 TNFa     out of range   100  98  
4 IL-6     out of range    77  75.5
5 IL-1b    out of range    74  72.5
6 MIP-3a   out of range    24  23.5
7 MIG      out of range    13  12.7
8 IL-8     out of range     4   3.9
9 IP-10    out of range     3   2.9

Key finding: IL-10 (100%), IFN-Y (99%), and TNFa (98%) are virtually unmeasurable — almost all values are “out of range”. These cytokines might be below the detection limit of the assay in vaginal samples.

Most out-of-range measurements are at a single value (below detection), but a few (MIG, IP-10, IL-8) have some very high values that are literally “off the charts” (>25,000 pg/mL).

4. Missing data

Code 
cat("No missing values in any table.\n")
No missing values in any table.
Code 
cat("sample_ids:  ", sum(complete.cases(sample_ids)), "/", nrow(sample_ids), "\n")
sample_ids:   102 / 102
Code 
cat("participants:", sum(complete.cases(participants)), "/", nrow(participants), "\n")
participants: 51 / 51
Code 
cat("qpcr:        ", sum(complete.cases(qpcr)), "/", nrow(qpcr), "\n")
qpcr:         102 / 102
Code 
cat("luminex:     ", sum(complete.cases(luminex)), "/", nrow(luminex), "\n")
luminex:      1020 / 1020

There are zero missing values anywhere. The README hinted that days_since_last_sex might have NAs, but it doesn’t. The data is clean.

5. Making a plot — and remaking it three times

Let’s zoom in on one question: Does total bacterial load differ between the yogurt and unchanged diet groups after antibiotics?

I’ll make this plot, get feedback, then improve it. The goal is not to create three different plots, but to make the same plot three times, each time learning something.

Attempt 1 — Raw plot, first draft

Code 
p1 <- qjoined |>
  ggplot(aes(x = arm, y = qpcr_bacteria, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_wrap(~ time_point) +
  labs(title = "Total bacterial load by group and time point",
       subtitle = "Version 1 — raw scale",
       y = "Total bacterial DNA copies", x = "") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
p1

What I see: The y-axis is dominated by a few extreme values. You can’t see what’s happening in the lower range at all. Most samples are squished at the bottom.

Attempt 2 — Log scale

Code 
p2 <- qjoined |>
  ggplot(aes(x = arm, y = qpcr_bacteria, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ time_point) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Total bacterial load by group and time point",
       subtitle = "Version 2 — log10 scale with individual points",
       y = "Total bacterial DNA copies (log scale)", x = "") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
p2

What I see: The log scale reveals structure that was invisible before. At baseline the groups look similar. After antibiotics, the yogurt group looks like it might have lower total bacteria overall — is that real? The jitter shows individual samples so we can see the spread.

Attempt 3 — Paired lines showing change within each person

Code 
# Reshape so we have one row per participant with baseline and after values
paired_bacteria <- qjoined |>
  select(pid, arm, time_point, qpcr_bacteria) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = time_point, values_from = qpcr_bacteria)

p3 <- paired_bacteria |>
  ggplot(aes(x = "baseline", y = baseline)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = arm), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = "baseline", xend = "after_antibiotic",
                   y = baseline, yend = after_antibiotic,
                   color = arm, group = pid),
               alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(x = "after_antibiotic", y = after_antibiotic, color = arm),
             size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  facet_wrap(~ arm) +
  labs(title = "Total bacterial load: each participant's change",
       subtitle = "Version 3 — paired lines, baseline → after antibiotic",
       y = "Total bacterial DNA copies (log scale)", x = "") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
p3

What I see: This is the most informative version. Each line is one participant. In the yogurt group, most participants’ bacterial load decreases from baseline to after antibiotics. In the unchanged diet group, the pattern is more mixed — about half go up, half go down.

6. What we learned

About the data

  • No missing values — the dataset is clean
  • All numeric variables are right-skewed — log transforms are essential
  • L. crispatus is undetected in 65% of samples but shows an interesting recovery pattern after antibiotics in the yogurt group
  • Some cytokines are unmeasurable (IL-10, IFN-Y, TNFa) — they’re below detection limits in vaginal samples
  • Extreme values exist — 9 samples with total bacteria >29 million need a closer look

Making one plot three times taught us

  1. Raw scale: useless when data spans 4+ orders of magnitude
  2. Log scale + jitter: reveals structure, shows individual points
  3. Paired lines: answers the real question — what happens within each person from baseline to after treatment

Biological hunches for Thursday

  • Yogurt might help L. crispatus recover after antibiotics
  • Yogurt might reduce total bacterial load post-antibiotics
  • Depoprovera use is imbalanced between groups — could that matter?
  • Several cytokines are undetectable — is that biological or an assay problem?
